html:
Johns Manville's Range Across Single-Ply and Asphaltic Roofing

Johns Manville is one of the few manufacturers with real depth on both sides of the commercial roofing market — single-ply membranes and traditional asphaltic systems — which makes their catalog a useful reference point on buildings where the right system isn't obvious from the start of the conversation.

Single-Ply: TPO and PVC (KEE) Membranes

JM's single-ply lineup includes standard TPO products in the thickness ranges we typically spec, along with PVC membrane options built on KEE-based chemistry, which the company markets for chemical and grease resistance relevant to food-service and processing buildings around the valley's ag corridor.

We treat JM's welded membranes with the same field verification as any manufacturer's — probing seams, checking fastening patterns against wind exposure, and documenting thickness at time of install.

Reflective color options run across the TPO line the same as with other manufacturers, and we bring that up on nearly every bid given how much solar exposure a Las Cruces roof takes on across a mostly cloudless calendar.

Fleece-backed variants within JM's single-ply lines add puncture resistance for mechanically attached recover applications, an option we weigh against fully adhered systems depending on the existing substrate condition.

DynaWeld and DynaGrip SBS Systems

On the asphaltic side, JM's DynaWeld and DynaGrip product families cover SBS modified bitumen in both torch-applied and self-adhered application methods. We bring these up on buildings needing the added mass and multi-ply redundancy mod-bit offers over single-ply — heavier equipment traffic, hail exposure, or an owner replacing an aging built-up roof who wants a modern equivalent.

Self-adhered DynaGrip in particular comes up on sites where torch work isn't a good option because of nearby dry vegetation or a tight fire-risk window, which is a real consideration on properties backing up to open desert lots around the edges of town.

Granule-surfaced DynaWeld cap sheets are available in reflective colors as well, which we bring into the conversation on any building where heat gain is a concern alongside the mass and redundancy benefits mod-bit already offers.

Traditional Built-Up Roofing Still in the Catalog

Unlike most manufacturers that have moved entirely to single-ply and mod-bit, JM still produces components for traditional built-up roofing — asphalt and felt systems. That matters on older buildings around downtown and the university area with existing BUR decks, where matching or extending an existing system can be more practical than switching membrane families entirely.

A multi-ply BUR system with a gravel or mineral cap still shows up on some of the older commercial stock near NMSU, and when a section needs repair or extension, staying within JM's traditional product line avoids compatibility questions that can come up when mixing manufacturers on an older asphaltic roof.

Insulation and Cover Board Products

JM's insulation and cover board lines round out a full roofing assembly beyond the membrane itself. On any recover or replacement, insulation R-value and proper drainage taper matter as much to long-term performance as the top membrane, particularly given how hard our sun works against an under-insulated roof deck.

Cover board selection also affects how well a membrane resists hail and impact damage, which is worth discussing on any building in an area that's seen hail with summer thunderstorm activity, since the assembly underneath the membrane contributes as much to impact resistance as the membrane itself.

  • Membrane category — single-ply or asphaltic — matched to building type and history
  • PVC (KEE) option evaluated specifically for grease or chemical exposure buildings
  • SBS application method chosen based on site fire risk and existing deck
  • Insulation and cover board specified with proper drainage taper
  • Weld or seam quality verified regardless of which JM product line is used
  • Warranty terms compared directly against other manufacturers at bid stage

Where JM Fits in Our Bid Comparisons

JM's breadth across categories makes it a natural comparison point on buildings with mixed roofing history — a section of BUR next to a newer single-ply addition, for instance. We still price it against Carlisle, GAF, and other manufacturers rather than treating any one brand as a default.

For a property owner managing several buildings around the valley with different roof ages and types, having one manufacturer that covers both asphaltic and single-ply categories can simplify sourcing repair materials and matching warranty administration, even when each roof still gets evaluated on its own merits.

We present the comparison in writing at bid time regardless of which manufacturer we're recommending, so the decision is grounded in the specific building's needs rather than a standing brand preference on our part.

Common Questions About Johns Manville Systems

Does Johns Manville still make traditional built-up roofing materials?

Yes, JM continues to produce components for built-up roofing alongside its single-ply and modified bitumen lines, which is less common among major manufacturers today.

What does KEE mean in JM's PVC membrane products?

KEE refers to a specific polymer chemistry JM uses in certain membrane formulations, marketed for enhanced flexibility and chemical resistance compared to standard PVC.

Is JM's SBS mod-bit different from other manufacturers' products?

The base SBS-modified asphalt chemistry is broadly similar industry-wide, with differences in specific formulation, reinforcement, and application method options between manufacturers.

Can JM products be mixed with another manufacturer's system in a recover?

Compatibility depends on the specific existing system and products involved — we verify compatibility on a case-by-case basis before recommending a mixed-manufacturer recover.

Why would a building with mixed roofing history benefit from JM?

JM's catalog spanning both single-ply and asphaltic products gives more options for matching or transitioning between roofing types on a single property without switching manufacturers entirely.
